Motion To Vacate Entry Of Sister State Judgment
Off Calendar Jan-30-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (Via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. COLE K. WALDHAUSER, Esq. of Foley & Lardner LLP (Tel: 213-972-4500)for Plaintiff;
Atty. MARTIN HIRSHLAND, Esq. of Dykema Gossett LLP (Tel: 213-457-1800) for Defendant.

Hearing RE: DEFENDANT AVICENNAS GROUP, INC. D/B/A/ ZENTIST's Motion To Vacate Entry Of Sister State Judgment.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court denies Defendant's request for a 60-day continuance. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Avicennas Group, Inc. d.b.a. Zentist's motion to vacate entry of sister judgment is denied.

Plaintiff Roboyo USA Inc. sued Zentist in Georgia superior court for breach of contract. After Zentist defaulted, the Georgia court entered final judgment against it for roughly $229,000. Roboyo commenced this California action to enforce the sister state judgment. Zentist now moves to vacate entry of the judgment under CCP 1710.40.

"[T]he judgment of a sister state must be given full faith and credit if that sister state had jurisdiction over the parties and the subject matter, and all interested parties were given reasonable notice and opportunity to be heard." (Conseco Marketing, LLC v. IFA & Ins. Servs., Inc. (2013) 221 Cal. App. 4th 831, 837.) CCP 1710.40 allows the court to vacate a sister state judgment "rendered in excess of jurisdiction," including where service was improper such that the court lacked personal jurisdiction. (See Law Revision Com. to CCP 1710.40; Fidelity Creditor Service, Inc. v. Browne (2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 195, 202-203.)

First, Zentist moves to vacate entry of the Georgia judgment on grounds it was never properly served in that action. (See O.C.G.A. Sec. 9-11-4(e)(1)(A) [requirements for service of foreign corporations].) The affidavit of service in the Georgia action indicates registered process server Rosemary Garcia personally served Zentist president, Atoullokhon Kasimov, at an address in Lake Forest, California. (Zentist's request for judicial notice, exh. 2.) Zentist submits contrary declarations claiming Atoullokhon lives in Newport Coast, California, and was never served. (Decl. Atoullokhon Kasimov, para. 1, 2; Decl. Max Kasimov, para. 1.) These declarations fail to cast doubt on Roboyo's evidence of proper service. In addition to the affidavit of service, the Garcia declaration credibly recounts that Atoullokhon identified himself upon receipt of service and that his appearance matched his description. (Garcia Decl., para. 6, 8.) Zentist's evidentiary objections to this evidence are overruled.

Second, Zentist argues the Georgia court acted in excess of its jurisdiction because the parties agreed their dispute would be heard in federal court, provided it had jurisdiction. This too fails. Both Zentist and Roboyo's state of incorporation is Delaware, and thus the parties lack complete diversity under 28 USC 1332. (See Roboyo's request for judicial notice, exh. E, F.) In sum, Zentist has not shown the Georgia court acted "in excess of jurisdiction" to justify vacating entry of judgment under CCP 1710.40.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
